figures.caption: Fig. 1. Comparison between traditional HOI settings and our U-HOI. Unlike prior art, U-HOI does not require training and eliminates the need for a predefined list of interactions. We tackle U-HOI using off-the-shelf Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), processing their free-form outputs and applying a post-generation refinement that extracts HOI triplets, all without being constrained by a fixed vocabulary.
Fig. 5. Evaluation of DHD [64] on the VG-HOI [64] dataset with and without the occurrence matrix M.
Fig. 11. mAP of MLLMs performance on verbs with propositions
Fig. 10. mAP of MLLMs on object manipulation verbs.
Fig. 8. mAP of MLLMs on opposite verbs.
Fig. 7. mAP of MLLMs on grouped verbs by topic.
Fig. 9. mAP of MLLMs of social interaction verbs.
Fig. 12. Qualitative results of LLaVA OV 0.5B [27] + AnyHOI + TT and CLIP [52] on the HICO-DET [5] annotated-box setting. Our proposed AnyHOI surpasses the CLIP baseline in predicting interactions.
Fig. 6. Results of LLaVA + AnyHOI + TT varying the number of generations on the HICO-DET [5] dataset under the annotated-box setting. Other methods using AnyHOI without TT are plotted for reference.
Fig. 4. Qualitative results of AnyHOI and CLIP [52] on the HICO-DET [5] dataset

Fig. 2. AnyHOI first detects human-object pairs using an object detector. Then, it crops the region encompassing both the human and its paired object and feeds it into an MLLM, along with a prompt to generate free-form scene descriptions. A post-generation refinement step then analyzes these descriptions to produce a relationship graph. Finally, the graph is refined by filtering out the triplets that are irrelevant.
Fig. 13. Qualitative results of LLaVA OV 0.5B [27] + AnyHOI + TT, and the intermediate outputs, on the HICO-DET [5] annotated-box setting.
